<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.96|> John Dunn was a priest of the Church of England, and in the 17th century, along with George Herbert and several others, one of the metaphysical poets.<|11.76|> <|12.90|> One of his poems is entitled Good Friday, 1613, Riding Westward.<|19.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Dunn begins the poem by describing devotion, or what we might call more commonly prayer,<|6.74|> <|7.70|> as the intelligence that moves the soul.<|10.90|> <|12.00|> As the poet's soul is moving westward, the poet approaches the setting of the sun in the west, of course.<|20.50|> <|20.80|> And it's there that the poet sees Christ on this cross did rise and fall.<|29.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So the imagery here is the rising and falling of Christ, the rising and falling of the sun, the sun setting and rising each day.<|11.20|> <|11.20|> And hence the circularity of light and darkness, the circularity of nights and days, the circularity, of course, of death and life in the vesper light that the poet sees.<|27.48|> <|27.48|> Amen.<|28.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But so that he doesn't get too taken with metaphysics, it's Good Friday after all.<|6.28|> <|7.02|> The poet returns his vision to the cross, writing,<|11.00|> <|11.00|> Yet I dare almost be glad, thinking these grand thoughts,<|15.78|> <|17.16|> and yet I do not see that spectacle of too much weight for me.<|22.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Again, the reality of the cross is just too much blinding.<|6.90|> <|8.12|> And so the poet scans the horizon and finds another character that takes his attention, namely Mary, the mother of Christ.<|20.30|> <|20.30|> Christ.<|21.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Upon his miserable mother cast mine eye, who was God's partner here, and furnished thus half of that sacrifice which ransomed us.<|12.58|> <|14.72|> Amen.<|15.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It's an incredible image, half of this sacrifice.<|4.34|> <|4.66|> The poet's being a poet, not a mathematician.<|7.54|> <|7.54|> It's not that he believes there's a 50-50 split on sacrifice between Christ and his mother.<|14.80|> <|15.82|> Instead, it's an image that calls to mind how there is actually something ordinary about sacrifice.<|24.44|> <|24.96|> Right?<|25.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Mothers make sacrifices for children, literally and figuratively.<|6.72|> <|7.78|> Human beings make sacrifices on behalf of others.<|12.02|> <|12.64|> The cosmos itself is in this circle of making sacrifices as death gives way to life and vice versa.<|22.00|> <|22.00|> Amen.<|23.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Sacrifice might best be thought of if you want a contemporary image in terms of sacrificial energy.<|10.48|> <|12.16|> Sacrificial energy is such that once it's offered, it creates more energy, more space, more grace, more creativity.<|23.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> even as it comes with a great cost or loss and always a profound letting go.<|6.96|> <|9.84|> Sacrifice is but one image we could associate with the cross.<|14.54|> <|15.34|> And Mary, of course, is only one character,<|18.42|> <|18.62|> although I find her to be the most profound of all the characters in this gospel scene.<|24.52|> <|24.52|> Amen.<|25.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But there are others.<|1.70|> <|2.90|> And undoubtedly, the most ironic character is Pontius Pilate, the Roman governor, the<|14.16|> <|14.16|> one who asks, what is truth?<|17.08|> <|18.34|> What is truth?<|19.68|> <|19.68|> What is truth?<|20.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> One of my favorite New Testament professors was interpreting this passage and got to that verse once.<|5.46|> <|5.56|> And he said about Pilate's question, why do the pagans get all the best lines?<|10.92|> <|15.36|> Pilate's the most ironic of all the characters because he asked the best question.<|20.54|> <|20.66|> What is perhaps the question?<|22.44|> <|22.44|> The question.<|23.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And yet he does not have the courage or the curiosity to wait around for the answer.<|8.68|> <|9.68|> As truth incarnate stands before him and hears his question and answers not in words but in deeds.<|21.62|> <|22.54|> In his very being itself.<|25.00|> <|26.00|> Amen.<|26.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Truth, sacrifice, are but two images that we could associate and rightly associate with the cross.<|11.14|> <|11.14|> But there are so many more, just as there are so many other characters in addition to Mary and Pilate.<|19.84|> <|20.70|> And that's where you and I enter the scene.<|24.76|> <|24.76|> Amen.<|25.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.88|> In a few moments, we'll have what's called veneration of the cross.<|3.80|> <|3.96|> Veneration of the cross is an ancient devotion that's a dimension that.<|24.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> You don't have to come forward one by one.<|2.72|> <|2.80|> You can come forward simultaneously.<|4.52|> <|5.48|> As you come forward, some will choose to reverence the cross.<|10.70|> <|10.94|> Some will choose to touch it or kneel close to it.<|14.20|> <|14.24|> You can approach it from any side.<|15.88|> <|16.88|> Some will choose to light a candle.<|18.80|> <|20.40|> Some will simply choose to remain in your pews and think from a distance.<|25.68|> <|26.34|> Okay?<|26.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But Thank you.<|1.00|><|endoftext|>

segments.text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Trying to look directly at the cross is like trying to look at the sun.<|7.66|>

<|11.16|> So let's look indirectly instead.<|14.06|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.96|> John Dunn was a priest of the Church of England, and in the 17th century, along with George Herbert and several others, one of the metaphysical poets.<|11.76|>
<|12.90|> One of his poems is entitled Good Friday, 1613, Riding Westward.<|19.90|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Dunn begins the poem by describing devotion, or what we might call more commonly prayer,<|6.74|>
<|7.70|> as the intelligence that moves the soul.<|10.90|>
<|12.00|> As the poet's soul is moving westward, the poet approaches the setting of the sun in the west, of course.<|20.50|>
<|20.80|> And it's there that the poet sees Christ on this cross did rise and fall.<|29.00|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So the imagery here is the rising and falling of Christ, the rising and falling of the sun, the sun setting and rising each day.<|11.20|>
<|11.20|> And hence the circularity of light and darkness, the circularity of nights and days, the circularity, of course, of death and life in the vesper light that the poet sees.<|27.48|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But so that he doesn't get too taken with metaphysics, it's Good Friday after all.<|6.28|>
<|7.02|> The poet returns his vision to the cross, writing,<|11.00|>
<|11.00|> Yet I dare almost be glad, thinking these grand thoughts,<|15.78|>
<|17.16|> and yet I do not see that spectacle of too much weight for me.<|22.60|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Again, the reality of the cross is just too much blinding.<|6.90|>
<|8.12|> And so the poet scans the horizon and finds another character that takes his attention, namely Mary, the mother of Christ.<|20.30|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Upon his miserable mother cast mine eye, who was God's partner here, and furnished thus half of that sacrifice which ransomed us.<|12.58|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It's an incredible image, half of this sacrifice.<|4.34|>
<|4.66|> The poet's being a poet, not a mathematician.<|7.54|>
<|7.54|> It's not that he believes there's a 50-50 split on sacrifice between Christ and his mother.<|14.80|>
<|15.82|> Instead, it's an image that calls to mind how there is actually something ordinary about sacrifice.<|24.44|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Mothers make sacrifices for children, literally and figuratively.<|6.72|>
<|7.78|> Human beings make sacrifices on behalf of others.<|12.02|>
<|12.64|> The cosmos itself is in this circle of making sacrifices as death gives way to life and vice versa.<|22.00|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Sacrifice might best be thought of if you want a contemporary image in terms of sacrificial energy.<|10.48|>
<|12.16|> Sacrificial energy is such that once it's offered, it creates more energy, more space, more grace, more creativity.<|23.80|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> even as it comes with a great cost or loss and always a profound letting go.<|6.96|>
<|9.84|> Sacrifice is but one image we could associate with the cross.<|14.54|>
<|15.34|> And Mary, of course, is only one character,<|18.42|>
<|18.62|> although I find her to be the most profound of all the characters in this gospel scene.<|24.52|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But there are others.<|1.70|>
<|2.90|> And undoubtedly, the most ironic character is Pontius Pilate, the Roman governor, the<|14.16|>
<|14.16|> one who asks, what is truth?<|17.08|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> One of my favorite New Testament professors was interpreting this passage and got to that verse once.<|5.46|>
<|5.56|> And he said about Pilate's question, why do the pagans get all the best lines?<|10.92|>
<|15.36|> Pilate's the most ironic of all the characters because he asked the best question.<|20.54|>
<|20.66|> What is perhaps the question?<|22.44|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And yet he does not have the courage or the curiosity to wait around for the answer.<|8.68|>
<|9.68|> As truth incarnate stands before him and hears his question and answers not in words but in deeds.<|21.62|>
<|22.54|> In his very being itself.<|25.00|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Truth, sacrifice, are but two images that we could associate and rightly associate with the cross.<|11.14|>
<|11.14|> But there are so many more, just as there are so many other characters in addition to Mary and Pilate.<|19.84|>
<|20.70|> And that's where you and I enter the scene.<|24.76|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.88|> In a few moments, we'll have what's called veneration of the cross.<|3.80|>
<|3.96|> Veneration of the cross is an ancient devotion that's a dimension of the Good Friday liturgy,<|8.90|>
<|8.90|> at least in Western Catholic churches, such as the Episcopal Church.<|13.60|>
<|14.68|> Everybody will be invited to come forward, if you so choose,<|17.96|>
<|18.36|> to come before a crucifix that Broderick will have brought in.<|22.02|>
<|22.12|> You can come down the side aisles or the center aisle.<|24.30|>
<|24.40|> Please know that.<|24.90|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> You don't have to come forward one by one.<|2.72|>
<|2.80|> You can come forward simultaneously.<|4.52|>
<|5.48|> As you come forward, some will choose to reverence the cross.<|10.70|>
<|10.94|> Some will choose to touch it or kneel close to it.<|14.20|>
<|14.24|> You can approach it from any side.<|15.88|>
<|16.88|> Some will choose to light a candle.<|18.80|>
<|20.40|> Some will simply choose to remain in your pews and think from a distance.<|25.68|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But whatever you choose to do, let us remember that what we do externally matters because it's connected with what we do internally.<|9.10|>
<|10.00|> What we think and feel.<|13.08|>
<|14.52|> What image that we discover and want to hold up.<|18.84|>
<|19.30|> Were some very personal question that we want to ask in the light of the cross.<|25.56|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.00|><|endoftext|>
